content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 14, 1991


Honorable Michael D. Antonovich
Chairman of the Board of Supervisors
Supervisor, Fifth District
County of Los Angeles
Room 869, Hall of Administration
500 West Temple Street
Los Angeles, CA 90012

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-476

Dear Mr. Antonovich:

This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding the permissible uses of campaign funds under the
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Our advice
is prospective in nature. We make no comments regarding past
conduct.

QUESTION

Under the provisions of the Act, may a candidate use campaign
funds to make a donation to the Glendale Symphony and receive
season tickets to the Glendale Symphony?

CONCLUSION

A donation of campaign funds to a civic tax-exempt, nonprofit
organization such as the Glendale Symphony is permitted by the
provisions of the Act. Accordingly, a candidate may use campaign
funds to made a donation to the Glendale Symphony and receive
season tickets to the symphony where the value of the tickets will
not have a material financial effect on the candidate.


1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. A-91-476
Page 2

FACTS

In previous years you have used campaign funds to purchase
season tickets to the Glendale Symphony. The Glendale Symphony is
a civic tax-exempt, nonprofit organization. You seek our advice
to determine whether you may use campaign funds to make a donation
of $580 to the Glendale Symphony which would include the value of
the tickets purchased. The actual value of the tickets you will
receive is $230. In the event you are unable to use the tickets
for a particular performance, the symphony would resell the
tickets and the value of any tickets so sold would constitute a
further donation to the symphony.

ANALYSIS

The Act provides that campaign funds may be used to make
donations or loans to bona fide charitable, educational, civic,
religious, or similar tax-exempt, nonprofit organizations, where
no substantial part of the proceeds will have a material financial
effect on the candidate, elected officer, member of his or her
immediate family, or the campaign treasurer, and where the
donation or loan bears a reasonable relation to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89515.) However,
such a donation of campaign funds is permissible only where no
substantial part of the proceeds will have a "material financial
effect" on the candidate, elected officer, member of his or her
immediate family, or the campaign treasurer.

While the Commission has not defined the term "material
financial effect" in the context of uses of campaign funds, it has
done so in the context of the Act's regulation of conflicts of
interest. Generally, these regulations provide that a transaction
has a material financial effect on an official if it increases or
decreases his or her personal expenses, income, assets or
liabilities by at least $250. (See Weiss Advice Letter, No. A-91-
158; Spain Advice Letter, No. I-90-410; Statham Advice Letter, No.
I-89-690.) Thus, in the absence of a Commission regulation on
this subject, we believe that it is reasonable to advise that the
proceeds of campaign funds will have a "material financial effect"
on the candidate or elected officer if an expenditure of campaign
funds resulted in a benefit of $250 or more to the candidate or
elected officer, member of his or her immediate family, or
campaign treasurer.2

The tickets you intend to use are worth a total of $230.
Should you choose not to attend certain performance, part of this

_____________________
2 Please note that the Commission will be considering this issue
at a later date. Our advice is, therefore, subject to change.

Our File No. A-91-476
Page 3

sum will also constitute a donation to the Glendale Symphony.
Accordingly, the benefit you will derive from your donation to the
Glendale Symphony is worth less than $250 and will not have a
material financial effect on you.

As stated above, a donation to a civic tax-exempt, nonprofit
organization such as the Glendale Symphony is permissible if it
bears a reasonable relation to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. (Section 89515.) You have advised us that
you believe that it is politically important to support local
community cultural organizations. Accordingly, it would appear
that a donation of campaign funds to the Glendale Symphony for
purposes of supporting cultural events in the community would be
an appropriate expenditure of campaign funds.

Staff is now in the process of developing regulations which
will clarify under which circumstances campaign funds may be used
to purchase tickets to sporting and entertainment events. In
general terms, staff's proposal clarifies that campaign funds may
be used to purchase tickets to sporting and entertainment events
when the candidate will participate in the event by making a
speech or other presentation, or by performing an activity at the
event such as by throwing out the first ball at a baseball game or
presenting an award or commendation on behalf of a governmental
agency.³ We encourage your comments on the proposed regulations
at any time before their adoption by the Commission.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.⁴

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Blanca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BMB:bb

Attachments


³ See draft regulations 18952 and 18953.2, copies enclosed.

⁴ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

sections.analysis:
The Act provides that campaign funds may be used to make donations or loans to bona fide charitable, educational, civic, religious, or similar tax-exempt, nonprofit organizations, where no substantial part of the proceeds will have a material financial effect on the candidate, elected officer, member of his or her immediate family, or the campaign treasurer, and where the donation or loan bears a reasonable relation to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89515.) However, such a donation of campaign funds is permissible only where no substantial part of the proceeds will have a "material financial effect" on the candidate, elected officer, member of his or her immediate family, or the campaign treasurer.

While the Commission has not defined the term "material financial effect" in the context of uses of campaign funds, it has done so in the context of the Act's regulation of conflicts of interest. Generally, these regulations provide that a transaction has a material financial effect on an official if it increases or decreases his or her personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities by at least $250. (See Weiss Advice Letter, No. A-91-158; Spain Advice Letter, No. I-90-410; Statham Advice Letter, No. I-89-690.) Thus, in the absence of a Commission regulation on this subject, we believe that it is reasonable to advise that the proceeds of campaign funds will have a "material financial effect" on the candidate or elected officer if an expenditure of campaign funds resulted in a benefit of $250 or more to the candidate or elected officer, member of his or her immediate family, or campaign treasurer.2

The tickets you intend to use are worth a total of $230. Should you choose not to attend certain performance, part of this

2 Please note that the Commission will be considering this issue at a later date. Our advice is, therefore, subject to change.

sum will also constitute a donation to the Glendale Symphony. Accordingly, the benefit you will derive from your donation to the Glendale Symphony is worth less than $250 and will not have a material financial effect on you.

As stated above, a donation to a civic tax-exempt, nonprofit organization such as the Glendale Symphony is permissible if it bears a reasonable relation to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89515.) You have advised us that you believe that it is politically important to support local community cultural organizations. Accordingly, it would appear that a donation of campaign funds to the Glendale Symphony for purposes of supporting cultural events in the community would be an appropriate expenditure of campaign funds.

Staff is now in the process of developing regulations which will clarify under which circumstances campaign funds may be used to purchase tickets to sporting and entertainment events. In general terms, staff's proposal clarifies that campaign funds may be used to purchase tickets to sporting and entertainment events when the candidate will participate in the event by making a speech or other presentation, or by performing an activity at the event such as by throwing out the first ball at a baseball game or presenting an award or commendation on behalf of a governmental agency.3 We encourage your comments on the proposed regulations at any time before their adoption by the Commission.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry. Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.4

CONCLUSION: A donation of campaign funds to a civic tax-exempt, nonprofit organization such as the Glendale Symphony is permitted by the provisions of the Act. Accordingly, a candidate may use campaign funds to made a donation to the Glendale Symphony and receive season tickets to the symphony where the value of the tickets will not have a material financial effect on the candidate.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
